An agent-based model (ABM) is a computational framework used to simulate the actions and interactions of autonomous agents, with the goal of assessing their effects on the system as a whole. In this case, the ABM is designed to replicate and understand the adoption behaviors of milk consumption by the British public from 1974 to 2005, focusing on the shift from whole milk to skimmed and semi-skimmed milk. The simulation results indicate a clear and consistent downward trend in the average weekly consumption of whole milk per agent over the observed time steps. Initially, the mean whole milk consumption starts at approximately 2654.81 units. This high initial value suggests a strong preference or habit for whole milk consumption among the agents at the beginning of the simulation.                                                                                                          Although not highly prominent, suggests minor fluctuations in consumption patterns but does not alter the overall downward trajectory. For decision-makers, these trends imply that interventions or factors influencing milk choice early in the simulation period have a substantial impact on reducing whole milk consumption. The stabilization periods suggest that after initial changes, consumption patterns become more resistant to further reductions, indicating the possible entrenchment of new habits or norms.
